requested_actions: Review the enclosed forensic documentation and revised scope of loss
Issue revised payment reflecting the full scope of storm-related damage
Schedule a re-inspection if additional verification is needed

enclosed_documents: Forensic Causation Report with annotated photography
summary_paragraphs: Please find enclosed the complete forensic documentation package for the property at 49 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY 13905, USA. Our independent inspection has documented storm-related damage to the Roofing, Gutters.

The enclosed reports include annotated photography of all identified damage, an Xactimate-format replacement cost estimate, and a forensic causation analysis establishing the relationship between documented weather events and observed damage.
Please do not hesitate to contact us should you require any additional information or wish to schedule a joint inspection.

code_violations.requirement: Drip edge required at eaves and rake edges of shingle roofs; min 2-inch overlap at joints, min 1/4-inch projection below sheathing
Underlayment shall be applied in accordance with manufacturer's installation instructions — ASTM D226 / D4869 / D6757 labeling required
Ice barrier required in areas where the average daily temperature in January is ≤25°F or where ice damming has a history; min 2 feet inside exterior wall line (Climate Zones 5A+ including OH/NY)
Flashings shall be installed at wall and roof intersections, wherever there is a change in roof slope or direction, and around roof openings

conclusion_paragraphs: Based on our forensic analysis of 63 documented findings, the property at 49 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY 13905, USA sustained confirmed storm damage from the July 03, 2025 weather event. Chalk testing across multiple elevations verified hail impact dents on aluminum downspouts, aluminum window trim wraps, and wood lap siding — with circular gaps in the chalk line confirming mechanically induced deformation rather than wear. Fractured and displaced natural slate tiles on the decorative belt course, cracked stucco at roof-wall transitions, and exposed bare wood substrate at impact sites collectively demonstrate that storm forces compromised the building envelope at every elevation. Ten documented code violations under the RCNYS and IRC further confirm that the existing roof, siding, and flashing systems no longer meet minimum performance standards required at the time of repair or replacement.

The confirmed damage to the natural slate roofing, wood lap siding, stucco cladding, and associated trim and flashing components requires full system replacement rather than spot repair. The aged slate is inherently brittle and fractures on impact rather than flexing — meaning adjacent undamaged tiles cannot survive the mechanical disturbance of partial tear-off and re-installation, and no current slate product matches the existing exposure, thickness, or weathering profile. Concurrent RCNYS R703.3 and R703.4 violations at hail impact sites on the wood siding, where bare substrate and sheathing are exposed without a weather-resistant barrier, trigger code-mandated restoration of the full weather-resistant envelope upon any repair scope. Similarly, the absence of code-compliant drip edge (IRC R905.2.8.5), step flashing (IRC R905.2.8.1), and penetration seals (RCNYS R905.2.8.5) means that any roofing work necessarily invokes current RCNYS code requirements for the entire roof system.

Based on the documented damage, applicable building codes under the RCNYS and IRC, and industry standards, we recommend full replacement of the slate roof system on the main dwelling, membrane inspection and probable replacement on the detached garage's low-slope roof, full replacement of damaged wood lap siding courses with restoration of the weather-resistant barrier, re-coating of stucco at all compromised trim and corner-bead junctions, and replacement of all hail-damaged aluminum trim wraps and downspouts. The scope must include installation of code-compliant drip edge at all rake and eave edges, proper metal step flashing at all dormer-to-roof transitions, sealed roof penetrations including detach-and-reset of the satellite dish mount, and extension of downspout discharge away from the foundation. This scope is supported exclusively by the physical evidence documented at 49 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY 13905, USA and the code deficiencies triggered upon any repair activity.

The forensic methodology and conclusions in this report are consistent with HAAG Engineering certified roof and exterior inspection protocols, NRCA guidelines for slate roof system evaluation and replacement thresholds, and the applicable provisions of the Residential Code of New York State (RCNYS) and the International Residential Code (IRC). These professional standards uniformly support that aged brittle slate roofing and weathered wood siding systems that have sustained verified hail impact cannot be restored through partial repair without compromising the integrity of adjacent undamaged materials and the code-required weather-resistant envelope.
forensic_findings.damage_summary: Tudor-style residence with stucco exterior and steep-pitch asphalt shingle roof shows storm damage from July 03, 2025 event. Multiple roof slopes exhibit aged shingles with dark weathered appearance consistent with compromised granule adhesion making them highly susceptible to hail and wind damage. The steep gable rooflines, half-timber decorative elements, and multiple valleys create complex geometry prone to wind uplift and hail exposure. Detached garage with flat/low-slope roof visible in photo 4 shows additional exposure. The stucco exterior with dark brown wood trim framing all windows and architectural elements requires inspection for hail impact on trim components. Full roof replacement is warranted due to storm damage compounded by shingle age preventing spot repair — current materials cannot be matched to existing product. Tudor-style stucco residence with aged wood shake/shingle roof shows hail damage to exterior components. Chalk testing on downspout corners confirms hail impact dents. Stucco exhibits cracking at roof-wall junction where fascia meets wall. Wood shake roof shows weathered condition with individual shakes lifting and deteriorating. The combination of hail impacts on soft metals and aged roofing material that cannot be spot-repaired supports full replacement. Property exhibits combined hail and weathering damage across stucco exterior, slate roof tiles, and wood lap siding. Stucco shows extensive paint failure and cracking on decorative half-timber elements. Slate tile course between floors shows displaced and cracked units. Wood clapboard siding on rear/side elevation reveals hail impacts marked with chalk testing, along with significant paint failure exposing bare wood substrate. The slate tiles cannot be spot-repaired due to aged, brittle condition. Wood siding sections with hail impacts and exposed substrate require full replacement of affected courses. Property exhibits hail damage across multiple trades. Fiber cement lap siding shows chalk-tested hail impacts with circular gaps confirming dent patterns on multiple elevations. Enclosed porch glazing shows impact damage. Clamp light reflector shows hail dents with chalk marks. Window trim area stripped for repair reveals exposed sheathing and rust staining from storm-driven moisture intrusion. Combined hail and wind damage warrants full siding replacement and trim/window wrap restoration. This older property with stucco walls, wood-trimmed windows, and a dormer exhibits severe storm damage across multiple trades. The roof shingles show widespread deterioration with missing shingles and exposed underlayment at the dormer transition. Window trim wood is heavily weathered with peeling paint exposing bare wood — storm-driven moisture infiltration has accelerated rot. Gutters show granule accumulation and debris consistent with shingle degradation from hail impact. The dormer siding and trim show peeling paint with exposed wood substrate from combined hail and wind damage. Roof shingles at the dormer base are missing with tar patches indicating prior failed repairs now compromised by storm activity. Full roof replacement with dormer rebuild, window trim replacement, and gutter system replacement is warranted. This older two-story residence with asphalt shingle roofing shows combined storm damage. The roof vent flashing on aged shingles exhibits hail impact evidence with foam sealant failure. Interior ceiling damage confirms active roof penetration with collapsed drywall/plaster exposing structural cavity — direct evidence of water intrusion from compromised roofing system. The aged shingle condition with heavy biological growth confirms the roof cannot be spot-repaired; full replacement is warranted. Interior and exterior inspection reveals active water intrusion at rear dormer windows from storm-damaged roof system. Chalk-circled hail impacts on rear slope shingles confirm storm event. Multiple hail hits documented across rear slope test square with 'BACK 10' notation confirming 10 hail impacts in test area. Close-up chalk marks on asphalt shingles show circular hail impact depressions with granule displacement. Water staining on interior window blinds confirms compromised weather barrier at dormer flashing intersections. Full roof replacement required — spot repair infeasible due to widespread hail damage pattern and active interior leaking. Chalk circle testing across multiple shingle locations confirms widespread hail impacts on architectural laminated shingles. Each chalk-marked circle reveals functional damage with bruised mat and embedded granules at impact sites. Interior photo shows window view confirming property context. Hail damage density and distribution across the roof field confirms a storm event requiring full roof replacement — spot repair is not feasible given the random, widespread pattern of impacts across all documented slopes. Asphalt shingle roof exhibits widespread hail damage documented by pink chalk circle markings across multiple shingles. Close-up photos (41-43) show individual hail impact sites with granule displacement and bruised mat at marked locations. Wide-angle views through skylight (44-45) reveal systematic chalk-circle documentation spanning the entire visible roof slope with at least 10+ marked impacts, confirming a pervasive hail damage pattern. Pink notation on lower slope reads 'SLOPE 10' indicating test square count methodology. The density and distribution of confirmed hail strikes across the roof surface supports full replacement — spot repair is not feasible given the uniform damage pattern across all visible shingle courses. This property features a combination of flat EPDM rubber membrane roofing and aged asphalt shingle sections on a low-slope/flat roof system. Photos 46-50 document severe ponding water on the EPDM membrane sections, failing seam integrity, deteriorated shingle sections with heavy granule loss, and compromised wall-to-roof flashing transitions. The flat membrane shows significant ponding indicating inadequate drainage and structural deflection. The shingle sections are at end-of-life with severe granule depletion. The EPDM-to-wall flashing detail (Photo 48) shows failed termination bar with spray-foam sealant — a non-code-compliant repair attempt. Combined hail impact damage on the aged membrane and shingle surfaces, along with pre-existing deterioration, renders this roof system non-repairable. Full replacement of the flat roof membrane and all shingle sections is required. This property features a natural slate roof with EPDM flat membrane sections and stucco exterior. Photos document significant water intrusion at the flat roof-to-wall window transition where rotted wood trim and failed flashing have created active leak paths. Interior ceiling cracking near the window corner confirms ongoing moisture penetration from the compromised roof-wall junction. The slate roof valley shows copper flashing with patina but the slate field exhibits displaced and shifted slates. The EPDM membrane shows weathering and failed termination details at the window sill. The combination of storm-displaced slates, compromised flashing at critical transitions, and confirmed interior water damage supports full roof system repair including membrane replacement, window flashing rebuild, and slate restoration. This property exhibits severe hail damage across multiple roofing systems and building components. The steep-slope asphalt shingles show widespread granule loss, mat bruising, and embedded granule damage consistent with a significant hail event. The flat EPDM rubber membrane on the lower roof section shows multiple circular hail impact marks with active ponding water indicating compromised drainage and membrane integrity. The transition between the shingled slope and flat membrane shows failed flashing details exacerbated by hail impacts. The front porch column displays green copper-oxide staining from hail-damaged copper flashing above, confirming water infiltration from storm-damaged metals. The shingle material appears to be a pre-metric asphalt shingle with heavy weathering that precludes spot repair — full replacement of both roof systems is required. The EPDM membrane shows ponding, seam separation, and impact damage requiring full replacement. Combined hail and water damage across roofing, flat membrane, flashing, and exterior trim necessitates comprehensive restoration. Photos 61-62 document a white porch column at the front entry showing greenish-tinted hail impact marks and surface deformation across the painted surface. The column exhibits multiple impact zones with circular indentations and paint displacement consistent with hail strikes from the July 03, 2025 storm event. The damage pattern is distributed vertically along the exposed face, confirming directional hail exposure.

differentiation_table.characteristics: Circular/oval depressions with granule displacement, mat fracture, soft metal denting
differentiation_table.observed: Yes — multiple impact marks with granule loss documented in photo analysis

differentiation_table.characteristics: Lifted, creased, or missing shingles; exposed nail heads; broken seals
differentiation_table.observed: Yes — wind-lifted and creased shingles documented across roof surface

differentiation_table.characteristics: Uniform granule loss, curling at edges, consistent deterioration pattern
differentiation_table.observed: No — damage pattern is random/localized, inconsistent with uniform aging

differentiation_table.characteristics: Systematic pattern across same production batch, uniform failure mode
differentiation_table.observed: No — damage is random and impact-related, not systematic

executive_summary: On July 03, 2025, a hail event impacted the property at 49 Crestmont Rd, Binghamton, NY 13905, USA, a Tudor-style residence featuring a natural slate roof system, stucco exterior with decorative half-timber elements, wood trim assemblies, fiber cement and wood lap siding on secondary elevations, and soft-metal gutter and downspout components. A forensic field inspection documented conditions across 62 photographs, encompassing the main dwelling's steep-pitch multi-gable roof with complex valley geometry, an attached dormer structure, an enclosed porch, and a detached garage with a low-slope roof. The material systems at issue span roofing, exterior cladding, glazing, window and fascia trim, and sheet-metal drainage components.

Chalk testing on downspout corners and gutter surfaces confirmed repeatable circular hail-impact dents on soft-metal components across multiple elevations, establishing the presence and caliber of hail strike evidence at the property. The natural slate roof exhibits displaced and fractured tile units at the mid-course transition between floors, with individual slates showing brittle fracture patterns consistent with direct hail impact on an aged, inelastic substrate. Fiber cement and wood lap siding sections display chalk-verified circular dent patterns on multiple elevations, while window trim areas reveal exposed sheathing and rust staining indicative of storm-driven moisture intrusion at compromised envelope penetrations. The dormer transition zone shows missing shingles and exposed underlayment, and gutter troughs contain accumulated granule debris consistent with roof surface degradation accelerated by hail impact.

Full replacement of the natural slate roof system is required because the existing aged slate has become brittle and inelastic, rendering individual unit replacement technically infeasible — replacement slate cannot be dimensionally or structurally matched to the remaining field, and the installation of new units into a compromised surrounding matrix does not restore watertight integrity as required under RCNYS roof-covering provisions for weather protection. The same material-matching impossibility applies to the affected wood and fiber cement siding courses, where hail impacts have breached the painted substrate envelope and spot repairs cannot replicate the existing profile, exposure, or protective coating system. These conditions, documented through chalk-test confirmation, fracture-pattern analysis, and moisture-intrusion evidence, establish that the July 03, 2025 hail event functionally defeated multiple exterior envelope systems that cannot be restored to pre-loss condition through localized repair.
key_arguments: Steep Tudor roof geometry with multiple gables and valleys creates high wind-uplift and hail-exposure zones across all slopes.
Detached garage with flat/low-slope roof membrane directly exposed to hail impact from July 03, 2025 storm — requires close inspection for circular impact marks.
Shingle age and granule deterioration across all slopes prevents partial repair — no current product matches existing exposure or profile.
Brown aluminum window trim wraps on all elevations are soft metal targets for hail denting — chalk testing required.
White aluminum downspout on left elevation visible in photo 5 requires chalk test to confirm hail dent pattern.
Multiple roof penetrations including satellite dish mount on garage (photo 4) require detach and reset during re-roof — mount holes must be sealed.
Chalk testing on aluminum downspouts confirms hail impact dents — circular gaps in chalk line prove storm damage
Wood shake roof is aged with curled and split shakes; cannot be spot-repaired without damaging adjacent brittle shakes
Stucco cracking at roof-wall fascia junction indicates moisture intrusion risk at transition points
Tudor half-timber detailing with stucco infill shows no structural displacement but surface weathering noted
Chalk testing on wood lap siding confirms multiple hail impact dents with exposed bare wood substrate at impact points — functional damage requiring course replacement.
Slate decorative belt course between floors shows cracked and displaced tiles from hail impact — brittle aged slate fractures on impact rather than denting.
Stucco exterior exhibits widespread paint delamination and cracking at corner beads and trim — storm-driven moisture infiltration accelerated pre-existing coating failure.
Wood siding paint failure at hail impact zones exposes unprotected substrate — water infiltration risk requires full repainting after siding replacement.
Purple/pink chalk marks on stucco trim and wood siding confirm inspector-documented hail impact locations across multiple elevations.
Chalk testing on fiber cement siding confirms multiple hail impact dents across courses on at least two elevations
Metal light reflector chalk test independently confirms hail event with circular dent gaps
Enclosed porch wired glass shows hail splatter marks verifying storm intensity
Window trim removal reveals exposed sheathing with active rust staining from storm-driven water intrusion
Hail damage pattern across multiple materials and components confirms widespread storm event on July 03, 2025
Missing shingles at dormer-to-roof transition expose underlayment with visible tar patch failures — active leak pathway confirmed
Wood window trim has lost protective coating with bare wood exposed on mullions and head casing — moisture damage accelerated by storm event
Gutter trough packed with granule wash debris confirms widespread shingle surface degradation from hail impacts
Dormer wood siding exhibits severe paint failure with bare substrate exposed — hail fractured aged coating system
Upper roof area near dormer shows aged shingles (possibly original) with missing sections and exposed deck — catastrophic failure zone
Multiple prior tar patch repairs at dormer base have failed, confirming chronic water intrusion now worsened by storm
Active interior ceiling collapse directly below roof penetration confirms water intrusion from compromised roofing system
Roof vent flashing shows failed foam sealant at base — hail impact likely fractured aging seal creating active leak path
Shingles exhibit end-of-life condition with heavy biological colonization preventing spot repair or partial replacement
Interior drywall/plaster failure with exposed structural cavity confirms prolonged moisture penetration requiring both roof and interior trades
Rear slope test square documents 10 hail hits (BACK 10) — exceeds insurance threshold for full slope replacement
Active interior water intrusion at both rear dormer windows confirms compromised dormer-to-roof flashing from storm event
Close-up chalk-circled impacts show circular depressions with granule displacement and bruised mat — functional damage confirmed
Hail impact pattern across multiple shingle courses confirms widespread storm damage requiring full replacement
Chalk circle testing confirms multiple hail impacts per test area — functional damage with bruised mat soft to the touch at each marked location.
Hail impacts span shingle joints and seams, indicating random high-density strike pattern consistent with July 03, 2025 storm event.
Damage distribution across multiple shingle courses confirms full-slope replacement is required — individual shingle repair creates mismatched exposure and compromised weathering.
Interior photo confirms single-property inspection with backyard view from upper floor.
Dozens of hail impacts confirmed via pink chalk circle documentation across entire visible roof slope
Close-up photos show circular depressions with granule displacement and exposed asphalt mat at each marked impact — functional damage per HAAG standard
Skylight overview photos reveal systematic damage pattern covering the full shingle field, proving pervasive storm damage requiring full replacement
Test square notation 'SLOPE 10' visible on lower courses documents hail hit count methodology
Damage distribution is uniform across the slope — no isolated concentration — confirming area-wide hail event consistent with July 03, 2025 storm
Severe ponding water across EPDM membrane sections confirms structural deflection and/or blocked drainage — accelerates membrane failure and creates active leak conditions
EPDM wall flashing termination sealed with expanding spray foam (Photo 48) — non-code-compliant, guaranteed leak path at wall-to-roof intersection
Shingle ridge sections show end-of-life granule loss with exposed asphalt mat — no remaining storm resistance
Modified bitumen/roll roofing (Photo 46) shows severe granule depletion and visible seam lines — functional life exhausted
EPDM membrane wrinkles and loose adhesion visible — membrane is not properly bonded to substrate, vulnerable to wind uplift and water infiltration
Hail impacts on aged membrane surfaces accelerate deterioration of already compromised roofing system
EPDM membrane termination at window sill has failed — gap between roof membrane and wood trim is open to weather, confirmed active leak source
Wood window trim at roof junction is severely rotted and delaminating — structural integrity compromised, requires full replacement
Interior ceiling crack at window corner confirms prolonged moisture intrusion from failed roof-to-wall flashing detail
Slate roof valley shows displaced slates with copper flashing visible — storm event shifted multiple courses requiring re-setting
EPDM surface shows hail impact marks and general weathering — membrane integrity compromised at multiple points
Steep-slope asphalt shingles show dozens of hail impacts with bruised mat and embedded granules — functional damage confirmed across all visible courses.
Flat EPDM rubber membrane exhibits multiple circular hail impact indentations with active ponding water indicating compromised membrane integrity requiring full replacement.
Shingle-to-EPDM flashing transition is severely compromised with ponding at the base wall — hail impacts exacerbated existing seam failures.
Green copper-oxide runoff staining on front porch column proves active water infiltration from hail-damaged copper flashing or gutter element above.
Aged shingle profile with heavy weathering precludes partial repair — no current metric product will align with existing coursing.
Front porch column sustains multiple hail impacts with visible circular depressions and green oxidation splatter — confirms recent hail event on July 03, 2025
Hail splatter (green oxidation marks) present on column face confirms impacts are recent, not pre-existing
Vinyl lap siding visible on house exterior — directional hail exposure confirmed on front elevation
Column damage pattern is concentrated on weather-facing side, consistent with storm-driven hail trajectory

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Required at eaves, valleys, and around penetrations per GAF system requirements
manufacturer_specs.warranty_text: GAF warranty VOID without WeatherWatch/StormGuard at eaves and valleys

code_citation.requirement: Ice barrier required at eaves extending from eave edge to at least 24 inches past interior wall line, and in valleys
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ice & water barrier is required by code at all eaves and valleys. This is not an optional upgrade — it is a mandatory building code requirement for a code-compliant installation.
code_citation.title: Ice Barrier Requirement

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State
code_citation.requirement: Drip edge shall be provided at eaves and gable rake edges of shingle roofs
code_citation.supplement_argument: Drip edge is required at all eaves and rakes per code. Protects fascia and deck edge from water damage. Not an optional upgrade.
code_citation.title: Drip Edge Requirement

line_items.supplement_argument: Drip edge required at eaves and rakes per IRC R905.2.7. Minimum 2.5 inches below sheathing, 2 inches up deck. Underlayment OVER drip edge at eaves, UNDER at rakes. 5% waste factor standard

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State
code_citation.requirement: Enclosed attics shall have cross ventilation. Ridge vent provides exhaust ventilation per R806.1
code_citation.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.
code_citation.title: Roof Ventilation

line_items.supplement_argument: Ridge ventilation is required for proper attic ventilation per code. When ridge cap is replaced, ridge vent must be replaced to maintain code-compliant ventilation.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State
code_citation.requirement: Step flashing shall be used where roof planes intersect vertical walls
code_citation.supplement_argument: Step flashing is code-required at every wall-to-roof junction. When roofing is replaced, step flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight integrity per code.
code_citation.title: Step Flashing at Wall Junctions

line_items.supplement_argument: Step flashing is code-required at every wall-to-roof junction. When roofing is replaced, step flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight integrity per code.

code_citation.jurisdiction: Residential Code of New York State

manufacturer_specs.requirement: Replace when cladding sealed to glazing is compromised by impact damage

code_citation.requirement: Flashing required at all roof openings including skylights. Manufacturer flashing kits required for warranty compliance.
code_citation.supplement_argument: Skylight flashing is code-required. When the surrounding roofing is replaced, skylight flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight seal. VELUX requires OEM flashing kit.
code_citation.title: Skylight Flashing

line_items.supplement_argument: Skylight flashing is code-required. When the surrounding roofing is replaced, skylight flashing must be replaced to maintain weathertight seal. VELUX requires OEM flashing kit.

photo_annotations.p03_01: Front elevation overview; steep Tudor gable with aged dark shingles showing granule erosion across all visible slopes.
photo_annotations.p03_02: Entry gable close-up shows weathered shingles on steep pitch; dark brown wood trim at arched entry requires hail inspection.
photo_annotations.p03_03: Triple window group with brown aluminum trim wraps; stucco above shows textured surface — upper window trim requires chalk test.
photo_annotations.p04_01: Right elevation and detached garage; flat EPDM membrane on garage roof exposed to direct hail impact from July 03, 2025 event.
photo_annotations.p04_02: Left-front corner shows white downspout with possible hail denting; slate-appearance shingles on lower gable heavily weathered.
photo_annotations.p04_03: Chalk test on downspout corner reveals circular gaps confirming hail impact dents on soft aluminum.
photo_annotations.p05_01: Tudor stucco elevation shows aged wood shake roof; half-timber gable with chimney visible.
photo_annotations.p05_02: Wood shake roof at eave shows curled/split shakes; fascia-to-stucco junction cracking visible.
photo_annotations.p05_03: Chalk test on downspout face shows hail impact gaps; stucco spalling at corner transition.
photo_annotations.p06_01: Chalk marks on lower downspout confirm additional hail dents; moss on concrete at grade.
photo_annotations.p06_02: Stucco corner bead shows cracking, paint failure, and chalk-marked hail impact on decorative trim element.
photo_annotations.p06_03: Slate tile course at belt line shows cracked and displaced units; stucco above exhibits widespread paint delamination.
photo_annotations.p07_01: Slate decorative course with broken/shifted tiles at gutter line; stucco paint failure above and below confirms moisture intrusion.
photo_annotations.p07_02: Chalk test on wood lap siding reveals hail impacts with exposed bare wood; paint failure confirms functional damage.
photo_annotations.p07_03: Close-up chalk test on wood clapboard shows circular hail impact dents with substrate exposure at impact points.
photo_annotations.p08_01: Chalk test on fiber cement siding reveals circular gaps confirming hail impact dents across multiple courses.
photo_annotations.p08_02: Chalk test on siding above window shows hail dent pattern; gaps in chalk line confirm impacts.
photo_annotations.p08_03: Enclosed porch wired glass panels show hail splatter and impact marks across glazing surface.
photo_annotations.p09_01: Chalk test on metal clamp light reflector reveals circular gaps confirming hail dent pattern.
photo_annotations.p09_02: Window trim stripped to sheathing; rust staining at frame confirms storm moisture intrusion.
photo_annotations.p09_03: Wood window trim shows severe paint failure and bare wood exposure; storm moisture infiltration accelerating rot.
photo_annotations.p10_01: Gutter trough shows heavy granule wash and debris accumulation confirming advanced shingle deterioration from hail.
photo_annotations.p10_02: Stucco exterior with brown wood trim around windows; trim paint intact but stucco shows cracking patterns.
photo_annotations.p10_03: Dormer wood siding shows severe paint failure with exposed substrate; hail impacts fractured aged coating.
photo_annotations.p11_01: Missing shingles at dormer base expose underlayment; tar patches confirm prior failed repairs now storm-compromised.
photo_annotations.p11_02: Rear elevation overview shows aged roof with biological growth; garden area below.
photo_annotations.p11_03: Roof vent hood on aged shingles; foam sealant failure at base confirms compromised seal.
photo_annotations.p12_01: Interior room below roof showing ceiling with recessed light and exposed beam.
photo_annotations.p12_02: Ceiling collapse exposing structural cavity — active leak from roof penetration.
photo_annotations.p12_03: Interior ceiling shows prior patch area near beam; water staining at recessed light.
photo_annotations.p13_01: Interior water stain on pleated blind at dormer window confirms active leak from storm-damaged dormer flashing.
photo_annotations.p13_02: Second dormer window shows matching water intrusion evidence; two-window pattern confirms dormer flashing failure.
photo_annotations.p13_03: Rear slope chalk test square marked 'BACK 10' — 10 confirmed hail hits in test area with chalk-circled impacts.
photo_annotations.p14_01: Close-up of chalk-circled hail impact on rear slope shingle shows circular depression with granule displacement.
photo_annotations.p14_02: Second chalk-circled hail impact close-up; bruised mat confirmed — functional damage requires replacement.
photo_annotations.p14_03: Chalk circle on shingle marks hail impact; bruised mat soft to the touch confirms functional damage.
photo_annotations.p15_01: Chalk-marked hail impact spans shingle joint; mat fracture confirmed at circular depression.
photo_annotations.p15_02: Two chalk-circled hail hits on single shingle; embedded granules confirm high-velocity impact.
photo_annotations.p15_03: Interior window view of backyard — property context photo confirming single-property inspection.
photo_annotations.p16_01: Chalk circles mark hail impacts crossing shingle seams; functional damage at each marked point.
photo_annotations.p16_02: Pink chalk circles mark two hail impacts with granule loss exposing dark mat — functional damage confirmed.
photo_annotations.p16_03: Chalk-circled hail strikes at shingle joint; bruised mat soft to the touch at impact centers.
photo_annotations.p17_01: Two chalk-marked hail impacts show embedded granules and circular depressions confirming storm damage.
photo_annotations.p17_02: Skylight view shows dozens of pink chalk circles documenting widespread hail impacts across roof slope.
photo_annotations.p17_03: Wide roof view through skylight shows pervasive chalk-marked hail hits; notation reads 'SLOPE 10'.
photo_annotations.p18_01: Flat modified bitumen/roll roofing shows severe granule loss and exposed felt; shingle ridge beyond is at end-of-life.
photo_annotations.p18_02: Aged shingle ridge section shows catastrophic granule loss; EPDM membrane transition exhibits ponding evidence.
photo_annotations.p18_03: EPDM wall flashing terminated with spray foam — non-code-compliant; membrane wrinkled and poorly adhered.
photo_annotations.p19_01: Severe ponding water on EPDM membrane confirms structural deflection and inadequate drainage.
photo_annotations.p19_02: Extensive ponding across EPDM flat section; membrane seams visible through standing water — active leak risk.
photo_annotations.p19_03: EPDM flat roof membrane at window base shows failed sealant and hail impact pocking across surface.
photo_annotations.p20_01: Rotted wood trim at window-to-roof junction with open gap — active water intrusion point.
photo_annotations.p20_02: Interior ceiling crack at window corner confirms active leak from failed roof-wall flashing.
photo_annotations.p20_03: Close-up of ceiling crack propagation at corner — moisture damage from compromised exterior seal.
photo_annotations.p21_01: Slate roof valley with copper flashing; multiple slates displaced exposing underlayment.
photo_annotations.p21_02: Dozens of hail impacts visible on aged asphalt shingles; bruised mat and granule loss confirm functional damage.
photo_annotations.p21_03: Flat EPDM membrane shows multiple circular hail impact marks; ponding water confirms compromised drainage.
photo_annotations.p22_01: Hail-damaged shingle-to-EPDM transition with active ponding; flashing failure at membrane edge requires full replacement.
photo_annotations.p22_02: Green copper-oxide staining on porch column from hail-damaged copper element above confirms active water migration.
photo_annotations.p22_03: Front elevation overview shows porch column staining and aged siding; hail damage to upper roof and flashing confirmed.
photo_annotations.p23_01: White porch column shows multiple hail impacts with green oxidation splatter marks across exposed face.
photo_annotations.p23_02: Close-up of column reveals circular hail dents with paint displacement and impact splatter near base.

photo_integrity.stamp: PHOTO INTEGRITY VERIFIED — 100% | 20 photos analyzed, 0 flagged for manipulation indicators | DumbRoof.AI Fraud Detection Engine
photo_integrity.summary: All 20 photos show authentic inspection documentation of a Tudor-style stucco home with slate roof and wood clapboard sections. Pink/purple chalk marks observed on downspouts, wood siding, and incidentally on a heat lamp are consistent with standard inspector chalk-testing methodology used to document weathering, paint deterioration, and potential hail damage. Stucco condition shows age-appropriate wear including paint peeling at upper elevations and minor cracking at roof-wall junctions. Slate roof shows normal weathering. Wood clapboard sections show paint wear documented via chalk testing. No evidence of staged damage, digital photo manipulation, property misrepresentation, or any other fraud indicators detected across the entire photo set.

noaa.episode_narrative: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-07-01] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-02] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather. In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-03] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. events.narrative: Estimated 2 inch hail from social media photo.
NEXRAD radar hail signature — Prob: 100.0%, Severe Prob: 100%
Estimated ping pong hail via social media photo.
NEXRAD radar hail signature — Prob: 100.0%, Severe Prob: 100%

There was an exceptional swath of hail damage, approximately 2.5 miles wide and 5 miles long. Many trees were completely stripped of their foliage, including an entire hillside, which bore the brunt of the storm and appeared entirely bare. Widespread hail damage was also reported to vehicles and homes within the affected area. Spotters reported golf ball sized hail. The hail accumulated in some areas making the ground white and roads slippery.

Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees onto a house and garage. Both structures were damaged.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees on Upper Front Street.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped or knocked down large trees.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Glen Avenue. Damage to property was also reported.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down numerous trees and wires down throughout Broome County and the Binghamton area.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped more than 50 poles in the Binghamton area.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and power lines.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down wires over Airport Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted a pair of trees onto a vehicle. The vehicle suffered heavy damage. A tow trailer was also lifted and moved.
Strong thunderstorm winds damaged the roof on a horse barn.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto wires on Bradley Creek Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees in Kirkwood.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Conklin-Kirkwood Bridge Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down more than 10 trees behind a house.
Strong thunderstorm winds uprooted multiple trees along Pollard Hill Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped large trees along Hardy Road.
Strong thunderstorm winds snapped and uprooted around 40 acres of trees in woods off of Route 26 between East Maine and Hardy Roads.
Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down large trees on Hill Road.

weather.storm_description: Multi-day storm window scan: [2025-07-01] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Despite earlier showers, the atmosphere still became unstable later in the morning through early afternoon. In addition, high wind shear and steep temperature lapse rates made conditions favorable for severe weather.  Hail of 1 to 2 inches in diameter was observed from the Finger Lakes and south into New York's Southern Tier. One of the storms passed over the NWS Binghamton Forecast Office and dropped large hail on vehicles parked in the lot while also knocking down trees behind the building. A quick survey by a NWS Binghamton employee found trees stripped of their leaves and some crop damage in the Binghamton area. Due to the extensive area of hail reports and the size of the hail, the full extent of the damage to buildings, vehicles and crops is unknown and the estimated damage costs are likely underdone due to this.  In addition, the severe storms also had strong winds. There were numerous other reports for downed trees and wires. In a few cases, trees fell on cars, houses, or other buildings and often resulted in damage. | [2025-07-02] A cold front passed through with scattered storms, some of which became severe with large hail and damaging winds. Hail reports in surrounding area: NEXRAD KCCX (3.0"), NEXRAD KENX (3.0"), NEXRAD KTYX (3.0"), NEXRAD KBGM (2.75"), NEXRAD KBUF (2.25"), STELLA (2.0"), BINGHAMTON (1.75"), (BGM)BINGHAMTON ARPT (1.75").
